name: Nimbarka
dates_notes: Sectarian dates much earlier (some traditions place him in the 11th c. or earlier); academic consensus 12th‚Äď13th c.
core_thesis:
NimbńĀrka's position turns on a single qualifier: *svńĀbhńĀvika*. Difference (*bheda*) and non-difference (*abheda*) between Brahman, *cit*, and *acit* are both real, but they are intrinsic to the relata themselves, not produced by any limiting adjunct or by any external condition (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 2.3.42‚Äď43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42-43), on the *aŠĻÉŇõa-adhikaraŠĻáa*). *SvńĀbhńĀva* is the term: of-its-own-nature ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 4 *svabhńĀvataŠł•*; *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 3.2.27](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/3.2.27)). Where BhńĀskara had qualified *bheda* as *aupńĀdhika*, holding it to depend on *upńĀdhi* and to fall away when adjuncts drop (BhńĀskara, *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 2.1.14](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.1.14), on *upńĀdhi-tńĀratamya*), NimbńĀrka refuses the qualification (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14, contrasting *svńĀbhńĀvika* with *aupńĀdhika*). The *bheda* of the *jńęva* from Brahman is not adventitious; it is constitutive ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1 *aŠĻáuŠĻÉ hi jńęvaŠĻÉ pratidehabhinnam*). The *abheda* is equally constitutive ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1 *hareŠł• adhńęnam*). Neither reduces to the other ([*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)).

The *DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* and the *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* state this directly. The *jńęva* is *j√ĪńĀna-svarŇępa*, *aŠĻáu*, *pratidehabhinna* ‚ÄĒ atomic in measure, distinct in each body ‚ÄĒ and *hareŠł• adhńęna*, dependent on Hari ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1, with the four-fold predication of v. 1ab‚Äďcd); the dependence is asymmetric, the distinctness is real, and both hold simultaneously in the *jńęva*'s very nature (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 2.3.18](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.18) on *j√ĪńĀtńĀ* and 2.3.19 on the *aŠĻáu*-measure of the *jńęva*).

Three reals are accordingly co-eternal: Brahman as KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa accompanied by RńĀdhńĀ ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* vv](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/vv). 4‚Äď5), the plural *cit* of sentient selves ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1; *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42), and *acit* as insentient nature ([*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.1.14)). *Jńęva* and world are *aŠĻÉŇõa* of Brahman in the substantial sense ‚ÄĒ dependent, controlled, non-different in essence ‚ÄĒ yet inalienably distinct as the parts of a whole that has parts of its own nature (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43), on *aŠĻÉŇõo nńĀnńĀvyapadeŇõńĀt*). The doxographic label *dvaitńĀdvaita* is later usage (so the doxographies of MadhusŇędana, *PrasthńĀna-Bheda*, and VidyńĀraŠĻáya, *Sarva-DarŇõana-SaŠĻÖgraha*); the school's own term is *svńĀbhńĀvika-bhedńĀbheda*, and the technical work is done by *svńĀbhńĀvika* ([*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)).

The operative soteriology is *bhakti-prapatti* to KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa within the Sanaka-sampradńĀya ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 2 *bhagavat-prasńĀdńĀt*; v. 4 *dhyńĀyema kŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáam*). Liberation preserves the *jńęva*'s real distinctness; it does not absorb the devotee into the deity (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 4.4.1‚Äď4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/4.4.1-4) on the *mukta*-state). The position therefore refuses two alternatives at once: BhńĀskara's *aupńĀdhika* qualification on one side ([BhńĀskara, *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* 2.1.14](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14)), on which difference would dissolve at liberation; and any Advaitic resolution of plurality at the limit (ŇöaŠĻÖkara, *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* 4.4.1, *avibhńĀga*-as-identity), on which the *jńęva*'s independence would prove to have been *vyńĀvahńĀrika* throughout.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Parijata-Saurabha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Brahma-SŇętra commentary; the Sanaka-sampradńĀya's school-defining bhńĀŠĻ£ya.
engaged_works.summary: The *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* is NimbńĀrka's *Brahma-SŇętra* commentary and is among the briefest of the major *sampradńĀya* bhńĀŠĻ£yas (Bose, *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* with English translation, 1940, intro., comparing length with the *Ňörńę-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* and *Govinda-BhńĀŠĻ£ya*). Its compression is doctrinally deliberate. Each *sŇętra* is read as licensing simultaneous and equally intrinsic *bheda* and *abheda* between Brahman and the threefold real of *cit*, *acit*, and *ń™Ňõvara* (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 2.3.42‚Äď43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42-43)) ‚ÄĒ the qualifier *svńĀbhńĀvika* doing the structural work that *aupńĀdhika* did for BhńĀskara ([BhńĀskara, *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* on 2.1.14](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14)), but in the opposite direction. The bhńĀŠĻ£ya is identifiably VaiŠĻ£ŠĻáava: Brahman is KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa with RńĀdhńĀ (cf. [*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* vv](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/vv). 4‚Äď5), and the soteriology is *prapatti*-inflected *bhakti* (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 3.2.24](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/3.2.24), on *bhakti* as *upńĀya*). Polemically the work positions itself against both *mńĀyńĀ-vńĀda* (*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 1.1.4, against ŇöaŠĻÖkara's *vivarta* reading of *tat tvam asi*) and BhńĀskara's *aupńĀdhika* qualification ([*VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.1.14)), but the polemic is largely exegetical ‚ÄĒ by terse contrast in the construal of disputed *sŇętras* ‚ÄĒ rather than by extended *pŇęrvapakŠĻ£a-uttarapakŠĻ£a* construction (so Bose 1940, intro., on the bhńĀŠĻ£ya's argumentative economy). The compactness has a structural consequence. It forces the load of detailed argument onto sub-commentary, principally ŇörńęnivńĀsa's *VedńĀnta-Kaustubha* (on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1).1ff.), which the Sanaka-sampradńĀya treats as the canonical reading and which subsequent commentators (KeŇõava-KńĀŇõmńęrńę, *VedńĀnta-Kaustubha-PrabhńĀ*, sixteenth century) build on rather than going behind. The *PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* is the school-defining text and the obligatory reference for NimbńĀrka-line VedńĀnta (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on NimbńĀrka).

engaged_works.title: Dasha-Shloki
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored ten-verse summary of NimbńĀrka's doctrine.
engaged_works.summary: The *DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* ‚ÄĒ sometimes circulated as the *SiddhńĀnta-Ratna* ‚ÄĒ is NimbńĀrka's ten-verse doctrinal compendium and functions as the catechetical statement of *svńĀbhńĀvika-bhedńĀbheda* (so the printed Chowkhamba edition, 1927, with the *Laghu-Ma√ĪjŇęŠĻ£ńĀ* commentary). The verses do four things in tight Sanskrit. They name the three eternally real categories: *cit* as the plural sentient *jńęvas* ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1), *acit* as insentient nature ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 3), *ń™Ňõvara* as KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa with RńĀdhńĀ ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* vv](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/vv). 4‚Äď5). They assert the simultaneous and intrinsic *bheda* and *abheda* of *jńęva* and Brahman without recourse to *upńĀdhi* ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1, *j√ĪńĀna-svarŇępaŠĻÉ ca harer adhńęnam*; v. 4 *svabhńĀvataŠł•*). They identify *bhakti* and *prapatti* as the means of liberation ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 7 on *prapatti*; cf. *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 3.2.24](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/3.2.24)). And they specify the *jńęva* as eternally an *aŠĻÉŇõa* of Brahman, never merging into and never being severed from its source ([*DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* v](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/v). 1 *pratidehabhinnam*; v. 2 on the persistence of *jńęva*-distinctions through bondage and liberation). Because the *VedńĀnta-PńĀrijńĀta-Saurabha* is so terse, the *DaŇõa-Ňölokńę* carries disproportionate doctrinal weight: it is the school's portable kernel, memorised and recited, and the structural template for the later sub-commentaries by ŇörńęnivńĀsa (*VedńĀnta-Kaustubha*) and KeŇõava-KńĀŇõmńęrńę (*VedńĀnta-Kaustubha-PrabhńĀ*; *Krama-DńępikńĀ*). For readers approaching NimbńĀrka's settled view in his own voice, this is the most direct first-person datum available (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on NimbńĀrka).
engaged_works.source_status_note: Sanskrit text not on disk in clean form; the wave-2 Vrindavan 1905 three-commentary edition does not contain the Dasa-Sloki separately. Citations should be sourced via Roma Bose 1940 Vedanta-Parijata-Saurabha vol 1 introduction, OR via Bhandarkar 1913, Vaisnavism, Saivism and Minor Religious Systems.

pada_analysis.gloss: fit for conjunction with and disjunction from a body

pada_analysis.morphology: particle; first component of sandhi cńĀnantam = ca + anantam
acc sg masc; second component of sandhi cńĀnantam = ca + anantam

karaka_structure.note: Object of ńĀhuŠł•; all accusative qualifiers agree with it.

key_passages.english_close: [Its] essential form is consciousness; and that [consciousness] is dependent on Hari. It is fit for both conjunction with and disjunction from a body. The jńęva, indeed, is atomic; and it is distinct in each body. They say that it is possessed of knowership and is unending [in number].
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the DaŇõa-Ňölokńę's compact definition of the jńęva: dependence on Hari, atomicity, plurality, bodily embodiment, and knowership are all stated without polemical overloading. Given that the on-disk bhńĀŠĻ£ya volumes do not expose a clean OCRable Sanskrit layer, this is the fairest securely transcribable locus for NimbńĀrka's ontology of the self.
key_passages.source_edition: NimbńĀrka, DaŇõa-Ňölokńę with Laghu-Ma√ĪjŇęŠĻ£ńĀ, ed. PaŠĻáŠłćit Dhundhiraj ŇöńĀstrńę, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

enaŠĻÉ vidur vai bhagavatprasńĀdńĀt |

prabhedabńĀhulyam athńĀpi bodhyam || 2 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: whose form is associated with beginningless mńĀyńĀ

pada_analysis.morphology: indecl; first component of sandhi athńĀpi = atha + api
particle; second component of sandhi athńĀpi = atha + api

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: anńĀdyńĀ mńĀyayńĀ pariyuktaŠĻÉ rŇępaŠĻÉ yasya tam

karaka_structure.note: Object of viduŠł•; muktaŠĻÉ, bhaktaŠĻÉ, and baddham are predicated of it.
Ablative expressing causal means: knowledge arises through the Lord's grace.
Object governed by the gerundive bodhyam.

key_passages.english_close: Its form is one associated with beginningless mńĀyńĀ. They indeed know this [jńęva] thus, by the grace of BhagavńĀn. It is spoken of as liberated, as devotee, and indeed as bound. And its great abundance of sub-distinctions is also to be understood.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the beginningless power of concealment and projection)
(roughly: a devotee; here a distinct soul-condition within the taxonomy)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is representative because it does not merely state that souls exist; it states how they become knowable, through grace, and insists on enduring distinctions among liberated, devotional, and bound conditions. That combination makes it a fair doctrinal summary of NimbńĀrka's anti-collapsing soteriology rather than a famous isolated line.
key_passages.source_edition: NimbńĀrka, DaŇõa-Ňölokńę with Laghu-Ma√ĪjŇęŠĻ£ńĀ, ed. PaŠĻáŠłćit Dhundhiraj ŇöńĀstrńę, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

dhyńĀyema kŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáaŠĻÉ kamalekŠĻ£aŠĻáaŠĻÉ harim || 4 ||

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: aŇõeŠĻ£ńĀŠĻáńĀŠĻÉ kalyńĀŠĻáaguŠĻáńĀnńĀm ekaŠł• rńĀŇõiŠł• yasya tam

karaka_structure.note: Primary object of dhyńĀyema; the remaining accusatives stand in apposition and describe the same object.

key_passages.english_close: From whom all faults have been removed, by his very nature. Who is the single mass of endless auspicious qualities. Who has the vyŇęhas as his limbs ‚ÄĒ the supreme Brahman, the most desirable. Let us meditate on him: KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa, the lotus-eyed Hari.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the ordered divine emanational forms such as VńĀsudeva and SaŠĻÖkarŠĻ£aŠĻáa)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the DaŇõa-Ňölokńę's direct identification of Brahman with KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa and therefore an essential witness for NimbńĀrka's theology, not a merely liturgical embellishment. It is also where the text most compactly joins auspicious qualities, supreme status, and vyŇęha-theology in a single doctrinal sentence.
key_passages.source_edition: NimbńĀrka, DaŇõa-Ňölokńę with Laghu-Ma√ĪjŇęŠĻ£ńĀ, ed. PaŠĻáŠłćit Dhundhiraj ŇöńĀstrńę, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

pada_analysis.morphology: acc sg fem pres part ńÄ

Object of smaret; the feminine accusative qualifiers agree with it.

key_passages.english_close: On his left side, [there is] always the daughter of VŠĻõŠĻ£abhńĀnu. Resplendent there, of beauty proportionate [to him]. Attended with delight by thousands of sakhńęs. One should always remember the Goddess, the granter of every desired good.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the daughter of VŠĻõŠĻ£abhńĀnu, inseparable from KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa in NimbńĀrka's theology)
(roughly: an intimate female companion in the divine retinue)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is representative because it makes RńĀdhńĀ's place beside KŠĻõŠĻ£ŠĻáa part of the statement of the worship-worthy supreme, not a detachable later ornament. For NimbńĀrka's living doctrinal profile, where devotion is constitutive and not merely preparatory, that is a load-bearing claim.
